title: Intelligence Briefing Dashboard
subtitle: Weekly Conflict Monitor · All Regions
sources: Reuters · AP News · Al Jazeera · ACLED · US Embassy Alerts · LiveUAMap · BNO News · ISW · British Defence Intelligence · Kyiv Post · Sudan Tribune · Irrawaddy · CNN · NBC · CBS · Fox News · HRW · UN News · CFR · Times of Israel · NPR · CNBC · Atlantic Council · Sudan Tribune · US State Dept · OHCHR · Wikipedia · Washington Post

regions.name: Middle East
overview: The US-Iran ceasefire collapsed on 7-9 July in the week's dominant regional event: Iran struck three commercial/crude tankers in the Strait of Hormuz, prompting roughly 80-90 US CENTCOM strikes on Iranian targets, which Iran answered by hitting US-linked bases in Kuwait and Bahrain, sounding sirens in Qatar, and firing 10 ballistic missiles at Jordan's Al-Azraq air base — Trump declared the ceasefire MOU 'over' before appearing to soften his tone on 9 July.

Khamenei's days-long state funeral (4-6 July) drew an estimated 10-20 million mourners, included a stampede that killed 8, and saw the procession cross into Najaf and Karbala in Iraq — a major cross-border religious-political event that paused Doha diplomacy just before it collapsed entirely.

Gaza's ceasefire continues hollowing out rather than holding — the cumulative toll since the truce began has reached 1,072+ killed per Gaza's Health Ministry — even as Hamas made real political concessions this week, dissolving its civil administration toward a UN-backed technocratic committee and holding weapons-handover talks with Egyptian mediators in Cairo.

Israeli territorial expansion continued on multiple fronts simultaneously: repeated settler attempts to cross into Syria's Mount Hermon buffer zone, new bases in Quneitra/Daraa, and West Bank settler attacks rising to roughly 6/day, while Syria absorbed a second Damascus bombing this week — two IEDs near the Four Seasons Hotel during French President Macron's state visit.

countries.name: Gaza
incidents: 6 Jul — Gaza-wide: Israeli strikes killed at least 6 Palestinians and wounded 20+ across Khan Younis and Gaza City despite the nominal ceasefire.
7 Jul — Khan Younis/Gaza City: Strikes killed at least 7 Palestinians including a child, hitting al-Mawasi, a tent encampment in western Gaza City, and a vehicle in Tel al-Hawa.
8 Jul — al-Mawasi, Khan Younis: Israeli air strikes killed at least 8 people including two children (ages 10 and 6), one strike hitting a displacement tent.

6-9 Jul — Gaza City/Cairo: Hamas announced dissolution of its civil administration in favor of a UN-backed technocratic committee; a Hamas delegation held 'crucial' talks with Egyptian mediators in Cairo on Phase 2 weapons handover.
countries.coa: The ceasefire remains formally intact but continues hollowing out — the cumulative toll since the truce began has reached 1,072+ killed and 3,463+ wounded per Gaza's Health Ministry, while Hamas's political concessions (dissolving its civil administration, weapons-handover talks in Cairo) suggest real movement toward Phase 2 implementation. CRITICAL maintained; watch whether Phase 2 talks convert into an actual reduction in daily strike tempo.

countries.name: Lebanon
incidents: 6 Jul — Nabatieh al-Fawqa: Israeli strike on a vehicle killed at least 4, including a school principal, her mother, a foreign domestic worker, and a Syrian national.
Ongoing this week — southern Lebanon: Israeli strikes on alleged Hezbollah sites continued at a near-daily tempo despite the truce now roughly 2-3 weeks old.

8-9 Jul — regional spillover risk: Trump's declaration that the US-Iran ceasefire is 'over' raised the risk Hezbollah could be drawn back into a wider Iran-linked exchange, though no major Hezbollah-initiated attack was confirmed this week.
countries.coa: Unchanged — continued lethal Israeli strikes despite the formal truce, and Iran's escalating confrontation with the US this week raises the risk Hezbollah is pulled back into a wider war even though it has stayed on the sidelines so far. CRITICAL maintained.

countries.name: Iran
incidents: 4-6 Jul — Tehran/Qom/Najaf/Karbala: Days-long state funeral for Khamenei drew an estimated 10-20 million mourners; a stampede killed at least 8; the procession crossed into Iraq (Najaf, Karbala) on 8 July.
6-7 Jul — Strait of Hormuz: IRGC forces struck a Qatari LNG tanker and a Saudi-flagged crude tanker near Oman's coast with projectiles; no casualties but significant damage.

7-9 Jul — Hormozgan coast: US CENTCOM struck roughly 80-90 Iranian targets (Bandar Abbas, Sirik, Qeshm) after the tanker attacks; Trump declared the ceasefire MOU 'over' before appearing to soften his stance on 9 July.

8-9 Jul — regional retaliation: Iran struck Ali Al Salem Air Base (Kuwait) and the US 5th Fleet base at Salman Port (Bahrain), triggered air-raid sirens in Qatar, and fired 10 ballistic missiles at Jordan's Al-Azraq base.
countries.coa: The US-Iran ceasefire has collapsed — the most significant deterioration of any tracked country this week, with active US strikes on Iranian soil resuming and Iranian retaliation now reaching four additional countries (Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan) within 48 hours, compounding an already-unresolved succession crisis. CRITICAL maintained; whether Trump's 9 July softened tone produces a new truce or the exchange continues escalating is the single most important indicator for the wider region in the coming days.

countries.name: Yemen
incidents: 3 Jul — Sanaa: Houthis threatened to attack Saudi airports and 'vital assets,' accusing Riyadh of blocking an Iranian civilian flight from landing.
4 Jul — Riyadh: The Saudi-led coalition pledged 'unprecedented determination and force' against any Houthi attack on the Kingdom.

4-5 Jul — Hodeidah: Houthi forces attacked government positions with snipers, drones and mortars in the fiercest clashes in years, briefly seizing ground before a government counterattack; 16 government troops killed, 22 wounded, government claims 50+ Houthi fighters killed.
5 Jul — ~30nm SW of Hodeidah: An unidentified skiff fired on a cargo ship with small arms; crew safe; attack unclaimed.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained — the deadliest Houthi-government ground clash in years, an unclaimed Red Sea shipping attack, and a live Houthi-Saudi threat exchange all landed in the same week, and Iran's own escalating confrontation with the US and Gulf states raises the risk Yemen becomes a secondary front. Close monitoring of the Hodeidah front and Red Sea shipping remains warranted.

countries.name: Israel
incidents: 3 Jul — Jerusalem: A 21-year-old resident was indicted for spying for Iranian intelligence, part of a wider pattern of Iranian recruitment cases.
4 Jul — West Bank: Coordinated settler raids hit multiple Palestinian villages near Ramallah; UN reports settler attacks have risen to roughly 6/day in 2026.

6-7 Jul — Mount Hermon: The IDF detained roughly 100 Israeli settler-activists attempting to cross the buffer zone into Syrian territory to establish a settlement; further attempted crossings followed on 7 July.
9 Jul — statements: Defense Minister Katz said the IDF is 'alert and prepared' to resume the campaign against Iran 'even for a third time' as the US-Iran ceasefire collapsed.
countries.coa: No strikes landed on Israeli territory this week, but the collapse of the US-Iran ceasefire has put Israel's own military on explicit alert for a renewed direct exchange with Iran, compounding escalating West Bank settler violence and repeated attempts to establish settlements inside Syrian territory. HIGH maintained; the Mount Hermon settler movement and any Israeli re-entry into the Iran campaign are the two indicators to watch most closely.

countries.name: Iraq
incidents: 4-6 Jul — Najaf/Karbala: The Khamenei funeral procession crossed into Iraq; Wednesday was declared a public holiday for the large crowds gathering at Shia shrines.

1-2 Jul (continuing into this week) — Iraqi Kurdistan: PDKI claimed an Iranian strike on one of its camps in the KRI, coinciding with PJAK-IRGC clashes in western Iran; the Khor Mor gas field halted supply to KRI power stations as a precaution.

Ongoing — Baghdad: Kataib Hezbollah and Kataib Sayyid al-Shuhada continue publicly rejecting the 30 September militia-disarmament deadline, while Asaib Ahl al-Haq and Kataib Imam Ali remain nominally willing to disarm.

7-9 Jul — regional spillover risk: With the US-Iran ceasefire collapsed and US strikes resuming on Iranian soil, Iraq's Iran-aligned militias pose an elevated risk of renewed attacks on US positions, though no new anti-US strike inside Iraq was confirmed this week.
countries.coa: Upgraded to HIGH. No confirmed militia attack landed inside Iraq this week, but the collapse of the US-Iran ceasefire and continued Iranian strikes into the Kurdistan Region materially raise the risk that Iran-aligned militias resume attacks on US positions in solidarity with Tehran. Force-protection posture at US facilities should be elevated pending clarity on whether the US-Iran exchange continues.

countries.name: Saudi Arabia
incidents: 3 Jul — Sanaa airspace: Houthis accused Saudi warplanes of blocking an Iranian civilian flight from landing, escalating into direct threats against Saudi airports.
4 Jul — Riyadh: The Saudi-led coalition publicly pledged 'unprecedented' force in response to any Houthi attack on the Kingdom.

6-7 Jul — off Oman: A Saudi-flagged crude tanker (Wedyan) was struck by an Iranian projectile in the Strait of Hormuz alongside a Qatari LNG tanker — the first direct Iranian action against Saudi-flagged shipping in this cycle.

Ongoing — Riyadh/Dammam: Several European carriers have suspended flights through 24 October 2026 over missile/drone threat; Saudi Arabia signed a defense-cooperation pact with Pakistan as part of diversifying its Gulf security posture.
countries.coa: Upgraded to HIGH. For the first time this cycle Saudi Arabia faces both a direct Houthi threat of attack and an actual Iranian strike on a Saudi-flagged vessel, a meaningful escalation from the prior verbal-only exchange. Hormuz shipping risk and the potential for direct Iran-Saudi confrontation are now live rather than latent.

countries.name: Syria
incidents: 2 Jul — Damascus (al-Marjah, near the Palace of Justice): A bomb at a cafe on al-Nasser Street killed at least 9 and injured 20; no group has claimed responsibility.
7 Jul — Damascus (near the Four Seasons Hotel): Two IEDs detonated during French President Macron's state visit, wounding at least 18; Macron was unharmed and no group has claimed responsibility.

2-9 Jul — Daraa/Quneitra: Israeli shelling continued near the 1974 line; Israel has established at least 10 new bases in Quneitra/Daraa over the past three months, in violation of the Disengagement Agreement.
6-7 Jul — Mount Hermon (Syrian side): Roughly 100 Israeli settler-activists crossed into Syrian territory from the buffer zone in a renewed attempt to establish a settlement.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained, trending toward CRITICAL-watch. Two mass-casualty Damascus bombings within a week — one during a head-of-state visit — plus continued unauthorized Israeli territorial expansion in the south constitute a serious deterioration in the capital's security specifically, even as Syria's political track (a newly seated 70-member parliament including Druze and Kurdish representatives) tries to project normalization.

countries.name: Egypt
incidents: 7 Jul — Cairo (Administrative Capital): Sisi inaugurated a new Strategic Command HQ showcasing counter-terrorism, electronic-warfare and anti-IED capabilities.
Ongoing — Cairo: Egypt condemned Iran's strikes on a Saudi-flagged tanker in the Strait of Hormuz as a threat to navigation security.
9 Jul — Cairo: Egypt hosted 'crucial' Hamas-Egypt talks on Gaza ceasefire Phase 2 implementation, including the weapons handover to the new National Administrative Committee.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. No direct security incident on Egyptian soil this week; Egypt continues in its consistent role as the region's diplomatic pressure valve, hosting Gaza Phase 2 talks even as the wider region grows more volatile around it.

countries.name: Jordan
incidents: 9 Jul — Al-Azraq air base, northern Jordan: Iran's IRGC fired 10 ballistic missiles at the base, used by US, German, Belgian and French forces; Jordan's military intercepted 8, with air-raid sirens sounding nationwide.
Ongoing this week — Jordan continued shielding its airspace against Iranian projectile transit as the wider regional exchange intensified.

Ongoing — King Abdullah continued routine economic diplomacy (Sun Valley Conference) even as the missile strike unfolded, reflecting an attempt to maintain normal governance alongside the security crisis.
countries.coa: Upgraded to HIGH — the most significant status change in the region this week. Jordan went from a quiet monitoring posture to being directly targeted by a 10-missile Iranian salvo on its own soil, with two missiles penetrating its air defenses. Continued hosting of Western military assets makes Jordan a plausible target for further Iranian retaliation if the US-Iran exchange resumes.

countries.name: Libya
incidents: 30 Jun-4 Jul — Sorman/Tripoli: Activist Abdelmoneim al-Mureimi, a prominent government critic, was abducted by Tripoli's Internal Security Agency and died in custody days later under disputed circumstances, sparking protests and a UNSMIL rebuke calling the arrest a 'kidnapping.'
2 Jul — HRW report: 'Libya: Anti-Migrant Rhetoric Fuels Abuses' documented ongoing abuses tied to anti-migrant political rhetoric.

Late Jun-early Jul — Washington/Tripoli: Eastern forces under Khalifa Haftar publicly backed the US-brokered unification plan; Saddam Haftar met Secretary of State Rubio to discuss unifying military, economic and political institutions under an 8-month roadmap toward elections, with PM Dbeibah retained in office.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. The US-brokered Haftar-GNU unification track is genuinely advancing diplomatically, but the al-Mureimi custodial death remains a serious rule-of-law red flag with the potential to reignite the unrest flagged previously — accountability for his death is the key near-term indicator.

countries.name: Tunisia
incidents: 8 Jul — Geneva: The UN Human Rights Council's 62nd session closed with experts and civil society 'sounding the alarm' over Tunisia's rights crisis, while UN member states largely stayed silent.

Ongoing — Gabès: The anti-pollution protest movement (roughly 2,500 protesters plus solidarity demonstrations in Tunis) continues, with 44+ still in pretrial detention and 25 NGOs branded 'foreign agents' by pro-government media.

Ongoing — judicial cases cited by HRW this week include Judge Anas Hmedi's conviction for defending judicial independence and a 25-year sentence for former Truth Commission president Sihem Bensedrine.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. No new violent flashpoint this week, but international attention on Tunisia's rights trajectory is rising (UN Human Rights Council alarm) even as most member states decline to act — the Gabès protest movement and the ongoing judicial crackdown remain the primary domestic risk factors.

regions.name: Ukraine/Russia
overview: Kyiv was struck twice more this week (2 Jul, killing ~22-30; 5-6 Jul, killing 19-22) even as NATO's Ankara summit (7-8 Jul) delivered €70bn in 2026-27 military aid plus €27bn for fuel-infrastructure — battlefield violence and Western commitment intensified in parallel with no ceasefire progress.

Kostiantynivka remains genuinely contested — Russia's claimed capture (3 Jul) is disputed by Ukraine's General Staff, and a rejected local-ceasefire proposal (5 Jul) underscores the city is a live flashpoint, not a settled loss.

Ukraine's deep-strike campaign reached unprecedented depth, hitting Russia's largest refinery at Omsk (2,500km from the front, 6-7 Jul) — removing the last of Russia's top-11 gasoline producers and compounding a fuel crisis now producing visible public unrest (18-hour queues, fights at pumps).

Moldova's government-formation clock is running (acting PM installed 8 Jul) but the pro-Russian Party of Socialists' boycott confirms the obstruction risk is materializing rather than merely feared, while Georgia's parliament adopted a new automatic-detention law further tightening its protest-suppression toolkit.

countries.name: Ukraine
incidents: 2 Jul — Kyiv: A Russian missile/drone attack killed at least 22 (some counts near 30) in the capital and surrounding areas, the third-deadliest strike on Kyiv of the war.

3-5 Jul — Kostiantynivka, Donetsk Oblast: Russia's Defence Ministry claimed capture of the city; Zelensky and Ukraine's General Staff deny it and say fighting continues; Russia said Ukraine rejected a local ceasefire proposal tied to the dispute.

5-6 Jul — Kyiv (nationwide): Russia's fourth large-scale strike series since 1 June — 68 missiles and 351 drones overnight — killed 19-22 (including 1 child) and hit military/energy facilities in Poltava and Dnipropetrovsk; ISW assessed the timing was chosen to generate informational effects ahead of the NATO Ankara summit.
6-7 Jul — Omsk, Russia: Ukraine's deepest strike of the war hit the Gazprom Neft Omsk refinery (Russia's largest, 440,000 bpd) roughly 2,500km from the front, disabling its main crude unit.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, arguably deepening. Deadly strikes on Kyiv are now near-weekly, Kostiantynivka's status is genuinely contested rather than settled, and Ukraine's long-range strike campaign has reached unprecedented depth. The 7-8 July NATO Ankara summit produced a €70bn 2026-27 aid pledge and €27bn for fuel-infrastructure but no ceasefire breakthrough — Putin continues to reject direct talks with Zelensky.

countries.name: Russia
incidents: 1 Jul — nationwide: The fuel crisis deepened further, with roughly two-thirds of Russian regions reporting supply issues and a daily gasoline shortfall estimated near 25,000 tonnes; June crude throughput was roughly 25% below a year earlier, the lowest in two decades.

4 Jul — nationwide: Rationing produced visible public friction, including reports of an 18-hour wait for fuel and drivers fighting at pumps; the government authorized lower-quality, higher-sulfur gasoline production through year-end to ease the shortage.
6-7 Jul — Omsk: Russia's largest refinery was hit by Ukrainian drones and forced to halt operations, removing the last of the country's 11 largest gasoline producers from the map.

Ongoing — Black Sea Fleet: UK Defence Intelligence reports Russia has fitted 3 of 4 Kilo-class submarines with counter-drone cages, a defensive response to Ukraine's extended-range strike capability, while its main naval parade again appears to have been cancelled over security concerns.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained; the fuel crisis is intensifying rather than stabilizing — the Omsk strike removes the last of Russia's top-11 gasoline producers from operation, and rationing-driven public friction is now a genuine domestic-stability signal. Putin continues to publicly downplay the shortage while sustaining the strike campaign against Ukraine.

countries.name: Belarus
incidents: 1 Jul — Minsk: Lukashenko pardoned 28 more political prisoners, continuing a pattern of releases aimed at easing relations with the West.

2 Jul — Bryansk region border crossing: A drone reportedly struck an intercity bus carrying 19 passengers near the Belarus-Russia border; the report originates from a pro-Russian outlet and remains independently unconfirmed.

6 Jul — Minsk: Addressing military-academy graduates, Lukashenko said Belarus faces a 'hybrid war' but vowed not to send Belarusian troops into Ukraine, while accusing an international 'war party' of prolonging the conflict.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Diplomatic diversification (prisoner releases, no-troops pledge) continues and the drone-relay truce with Ukraine is holding, but both sides describe it as fragile, and the unconfirmed Bryansk bus-drone incident is worth monitoring for corroboration.

countries.name: Moldova
incidents: 3 Jul — Chișinău: PM Alexandru Munteanu resigned after eight months in office, triggering full government resignation amid the MoldATSA corruption scandal.
6 Jul — Chișinău: The pro-Russian Party of Socialists formally announced it will not participate in President Sandu's consultations on a new PM candidate.

8 Jul — Chișinău: Deputy PM Eugeniu Osmochescu was sworn in as Acting Prime Minister by presidential decree for the transition period, with formal party consultations on a full nominee beginning this week.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. The government-formation clock is now running — an acting PM is in place and consultations have begun — but the Socialists' confirmed boycott shows the obstruction risk flagged previously is materializing rather than merely feared. The outcome over the next 1-2 weeks remains the key indicator.

countries.name: Georgia
incidents: 7 Jul — Tbilisi: Pro-EU protests reached their 587th consecutive day outside parliament despite heavy rain, still demanding release of political prisoners and a return to the Euro-Atlantic course.

Early-mid Jul — Tbilisi: Parliament adopted a new law imposing automatic 30-60 day detention on anyone with unpaid prior fines who commits even a minor assembly-related offense, further tightening the protest-suppression legal framework.

Ongoing — Brussels: EU foreign ministers' sanctions debate over Georgia remains stalled due to Hungarian and Slovak vetoes, even as more than 10 member states continue pushing for targeted sanctions on judges tied to opposition arrests.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. The new automatic-detention law is a genuine escalation in the state's legal toolkit against protesters, while EU sanctions remain gridlocked — the gap between rhetorical Western pressure and actual enforcement continues to widen.

countries.name: Armenia
incidents: 6 Jul — Yekaterinburg, Russia: PM Pashinyan made his first visit to Russia since June's parliamentary election, meeting PM Mishustin at the Innoprom exhibition, described by observers as a test of bilateral relations rather than a breakthrough.

Early Jul — Moscow/Yerevan: Russian FM Lavrov said CSTO members have agreed to consider formally invoking the bloc's charter over Armenia's unpaid dues; Pashinyan reiterated Armenia does not plan to change its posture and has already 'irrevocably' broken with the bloc in practice.
Ongoing — Azerbaijan continued supplying Armenia with gasoline and diesel as a normalization step, even as the formal peace treaty remains unsigned pending Armenia's constitutional referendum.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. No new security incident, but the CSTO expulsion question has moved from rhetorical to procedural, a genuine diplomatic escalation worth tracking even though the fuel-supply normalization with Azerbaijan continues in parallel.

countries.name: Azerbaijan
incidents: 1 Jul — Baku: European Commission President von der Leyen congratulated Aliyev on the initialed Armenia peace agreement and announced up to €200 million in EU Global Gateway grants for South Caucasus transport, energy and digital links.
4 Jul — Baku: Aliyev stated Azerbaijan and Armenia now 'live in peace' and are developing trade ties despite the border remaining formally closed pending Armenia's constitutional changes.
7-8 Jul — Baku: Aliyev held meetings with a US Senate Foreign Relations Committee member and Jordan's Deputy PM/FM, continuing routine diplomatic engagement.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. No security incidents; continued diplomatic and economic normalization momentum with both the EU and Armenia. The stalled constitutional-referendum requirement in Armenia remains the principal slow-moving political risk rather than an acute security one.

regions.name: Sahel
overview: El Obeid has not fallen but its encirclement tightened further this week — UN rights chief Volker Türk issued a 'red alert' explicitly comparing it to El Fasher's 2025 massacre, and the UN Human Rights Council adopted a resolution ordering an urgent independent inquiry; the gap between escalating rhetoric and actual protective action remains the defining feature of the siege.

Mali's Anéfis siege is the most consequential single development region-wide — JNIM and the Azawad Liberation Front relaunched a coordinated multi-front offensive on 4 July, ambushing a relief convoy and downing a helicopter, putting one of the junta/Africa Corps' key 2023-era territorial gains genuinely at risk of falling.

Niger's reported military mutiny over unresourced Tillabéri deployments moved from unconfirmed to multiply-sourced this week, without any official government acknowledgment — a coup-risk indicator given the junta's own 2023 origins, layered on continued JNIM/IS-Sahel activity in the deadliest area of the central Sahel for civilians.

Burkina Faso and Chad again produced almost no independently verifiable incident reporting this week — a deliberate information-control effect rather than evidence of calm — while Nigeria's Borno abduction crisis remains unresolved despite a string of small-scale rescues.

countries.name: Mali
incidents: 4 Jul — Aguelhok, Anéfis, Gao, Sévaré, Kéniéroba: JNIM and the Azawad Liberation Front relaunched a coordinated multi-front offensive on army/Africa Corps positions across northern, central and southern Mali after a roughly two-month lull; a prison complex near Bamako was set ablaze at Kéniéroba, and the army claims 20 militants killed at Sévaré, 6 at Gao.

5 Jul — Anéfis: FAMa and Africa Corps troops came under siege after JNIM-FLA fighters ambushed a relief convoy from Gao and shot down a Mi-24 helicopter near Tabrichat; Anéfis, one of the junta's biggest 2023-era territorial gains, remained besieged as of 8 July.

1 Jul (pre-window context, ongoing pattern) — Niono, Molodo, Sandaré, Nioro du Sahel, Gogui, Kayes, Diboli: Seven simultaneous JNIM attacks hit western Mali near the Mauritanian border, underscoring the offensive's geographic breadth.
Ongoing — Bamako approaches: JNIM's fuel blockade continues to strangle the capital with no lifting reported; fuel queues and reduced city lighting persist.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, trending worse. The Anéfis siege is a genuine strategic-reversal risk — a key Africa Corps/FAMa territorial gain from 2023 is now directly threatened — and the Kéniéroba prison attack shows JNIM-FLA reach extending toward the capital's approaches. This is sustained siege warfare compounded by an expanding two-front insurgency.

countries.name: Niger
incidents: 3-4 Jul — Niamey: Multiply-sourced reports describe a limited mutiny by an armored unit refusing deployment to frontline Tillabéri positions (Inatès, Chinagodrar) over inadequate equipment; the Presidential Guard reinforced the palace with armored vehicles overnight, though the government has issued no official statement.
30 Jun (context underpinning this week) — Domol Leydi, Tillabéri: 47 killed, including 33 soldiers, in the immediate precursor attack to the mutiny reports.
Ongoing — Tillabéri region: 223 military, militia and civilian deaths were recorded across 13 major incidents in June alone, the deadliest area of the central Sahel for civilians.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. What was an unconfirmed mutiny last week is now substantiated by named-source reporting, though Niamey has still issued no official acknowledgment — this should be treated as a genuine political-military stress event and a coup-risk indicator given the pattern of prior Sahel juntas, layered on continued high-casualty JNIM/IS-Sahel activity.

countries.name: Burkina Faso
incidents: 6 Jul — Djibo: A New Humanitarian investigation documented Burkinabè army units actively preventing civilians from leaving jihadist-besieged towns, with soldiers threatening residents attempting to join evacuation convoys — described by aid workers as a 'double blockade' of jihadist siege plus government movement restriction.

Ongoing — nationwide: No independently verifiable new kinetic incident with a specific date was found this week despite extensive searching, consistent with continued internet blackouts and journalist detentions suppressing outward news flow.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained on the sustained structural crisis. The continued near-total absence of fresh sourced tactical reporting is very likely an information-blackout effect rather than genuine de-escalation, reinforced this week by direct evidence the junta is restricting civilian movement out of besieged towns like Djibo to obscure the scale of displacement.

countries.name: Sudan
incidents: 3 Jul — El Obeid: UN rights chief Volker Türk issued a 'red alert' warning of imminent atrocities, explicitly comparing the city's encirclement to El Fasher's 2025 massacre; a Joint Darfur Force fuel convoy clashed with RSF forces nearby the same day.
5 Jul — El Obeid: An RSF-SAF clash near the city killed a child and injured four others.

6-7 Jul — El Obeid: RSF now controls all routes into the city except the eastern approach; roughly 500,000 residents remain trapped (105,000 already displaced within the city), which has been without electricity for nearly a month after drone strikes hit its power substation; food prices are up to 300% and water costs have doubled.

This week — Geneva: The UN Human Rights Council adopted a resolution ordering an urgent independent inquiry into atrocities around El Obeid, following reports that ten consecutive days of drone strikes killed at least 50 civilians in the city and North Kordofan.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. El Obeid has not fallen, but its encirclement has tightened materially and it is now explicitly framed by UN officials as 'Sudan's next El Fasher' — the gap between rhetorical alarm (red alert, Human Rights Council inquiry) and actual protective action (no ceasefire, no safe corridor) remains the key thing to watch in the coming weeks.

countries.name: Chad
incidents: Ongoing — Lake Chad basin: UN reporting documents an 80% rise in security incidents since January 2024, with roughly 1,800 incidents and 5,700+ deaths basin-wide since September 2025 and roughly 60,000 displaced within Chad's Lac province; the state of emergency declared 8 May remains in force.

Ongoing — no specific new dated Boko Haram/ISWAP attack on Chadian territory was confirmed this week despite extensive searching; the most recent confirmed incident remains the 4-6 May Barka Tolorom attack.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. No new confirmed incident inside Chad this week, reflecting thin open-source coverage rather than de-escalation — the underlying basin-wide ISWAP/Boko Haram turf war and the active state of emergency remain unresolved, and investment in early-warning reporting continues to be warranted given the persistent information gap.

countries.name: Mauritania
incidents: Ongoing — Mali border: The underlying Yélimané-area friction over FAMA/Africa Corps operations accused of killing Mauritanian civilians and Fulani community members continues unresolved, though no new dated incident inside Mauritania was confirmed this week.
Ongoing — Mbera camp, Hodh El Chargui: Malian-refugee inflows continue to exceed camp capacity by roughly 40,000, compounding cross-border strain from Mali's deepening crisis.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Direct domestic incidents remain rare this week; this is a genuine reporting gap rather than de-escalation — the underlying Mali-border friction driver has not resolved, it simply did not generate fresh dated news in this window.

countries.name: Nigeria
incidents: 1 Jul — Mairari FOB, Guzamala LGA, Borno: An ISWAP night attack aimed at disrupting the resettlement of 950+ returned households was repelled, killing 2 soldiers and 1 CJTF member.

5-7 Jul — Talakawa/Chibok, Askira/Uba LGA, Borno: Troops carried out a string of small-scale rescues — six abductees (4 adults, 2 infants) on 5 July, two abducted girls on 6 July, and two more abductees plus recovered cash/logistics on 7 July — but the bulk of the roughly 36-37 students taken in the 29 June Lassa school abduction remain unaccounted for.

9 Jul — Borno: Six ISWAP fighters and family members surrendered to troops even as insurgents mounted a coordinated attack on FOB Logomani in Ngala LGA, briefly breaching the perimeter before being repelled.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. This week brought incremental, piecemeal recovery — small rescue operations and some ISWAP surrenders — rather than a breakthrough; the bulk of the Lassa school abductees remain missing and ISWAP retains offensive capacity, as shown by the Logomani base breach. Sustained operational pressure and hardened school security in Borno remain required.

regions.name: Asia-Pacific
overview: China ran a five-front simultaneous grey-zone pressure campaign this week — a Senkaku coast-guard face-off and continued export controls against Japan, a 50-vessel swarm near Sabina/Iroquois Reef against the Philippines, sustained ADIZ incursions against Taiwan, joint China-Russia 'Joint Sea-2026' drills monitored by South Korea, and — the most significant new escalation — its first-ever open-ocean submarine-launched ballistic missile test into the South Pacific, drawing condemnation from New Zealand, Australia, Japan and the US.

Pakistan's conflict with the Afghan Taliban broadened this week beyond the border strip into a major internal Balochistan insurgency surge, with TTP-allied and Balochistan Liberation Army attacks killing 42 in a single week even as more than 66,000 Afghans were expelled from Pakistan in roughly a month.

Myanmar's Kyaukphyu SEZ is now the decisive flashpoint of the Rakhine campaign — the Arakan Army is actively assaulting the naval base defending the China-backed pipeline and deep-sea port, while the junta has surged reinforcements specifically to protect Chinese infrastructure, raising direct China-AA friction risk as the AA's 2027 'final victory' timeline approaches.

North Korea's DMZ fortification continues on an accelerated timeline (fencing now within 80m of the MDL) while Pyongyang has begun explicitly framing deepening Seoul-Tokyo defense cooperation as an existential threat — a rhetorical shift that may be laying groundwork for further provocations.

countries.name: North Korea
incidents: Ongoing — DMZ: Fortification continues on an accelerated timeline, with fencing now within roughly 80m of the Military Demarcation Line and roughly 90km of fencing and 70km of tactical roads completed along the 250km line; UN Command and Seoul remain in open disagreement over whether this breaches the armistice.

3 Jul — Sea of Japan/West Sea: North Korea conducted weapons tests for its new guided-missile destroyer Kang Kon, evaluating strategic cruise-missile launch capability, following the June commissioning of its sister ship Choe Hyon.

9 Jul — Pyongyang: KCNA condemned expanding South Korea-Japan military cooperation, including a prospective logistics-sharing agreement, as a 'foolish act courting self-destruction,' reacting to a Trump-Lee meeting on shipbuilding cooperation at the NATO summit.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. DMZ fortification continues on an accelerated timeline, the destroyer program is advancing operational tests, and Pyongyang is now explicitly framing Seoul-Tokyo defense cooperation as an escalatory threat — enhanced ISR coverage of DMZ activity and continued joint ROK-US monitoring remain warranted.

countries.name: Myanmar
incidents: 3-9 Jul — Sittwe: The Arakan Army pressed attacks on the junta's Shwe Min Gan naval base and Regional Operations Command outposts, with fighting now within roughly 2km of the town center and residents reporting daily artillery exchanges.

3-9 Jul — Kyaukphyu: AA forces attacked the Taung Maw Oo naval base defending the China-backed SEZ; the junta reinforced with 200+ additional troops, tanks and naval vessels specifically to protect the Chinese-backed infrastructure.
1 Jul — Kyauktaw: The AA launched its 'Arakan Lottery,' formalizing its parallel-state economic and welfare apparatus with 50% of revenue earmarked for health and education.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, arguably intensifying. Kyaukphyu is now the decisive flashpoint of the Rakhine campaign — a Chinese-protected SEZ sits inside an active AA siege — raising direct China-AA friction risk as the AA's Commander-in-Chief reiterates a vow to capture Sittwe, Kyaukphyu and Manaung by 2027.

countries.name: Pakistan
incidents: 1 Jul — border-wide: Afghanistan fired drones into Pakistan, continuing the tit-for-tat cycle following the prior week's Karachi Rangers HQ attack and Pakistani retaliatory strikes into Afghan territory.

5-8 Jul — Balochistan: TTP-allied and Balochistan Liberation Army fighters killed 18 police officers and 11 soldiers (42 dead total including civilians) in a wave of attacks; security forces claim 54 militants killed in response operations.
30 May-2 Jul — Torkham/Spin Boldak/Bahramcha: More than 66,000 Afghans were expelled from Pakistan in roughly one month, most forcibly returned, per Taliban migrant-affairs reporting.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The conflict has broadened beyond the Pakistan-Afghanistan border strip into a major internal Balochistan insurgency surge — 42 dead in a matter of days — suggesting risk of further diffusion rather than de-escalation; renewed third-party mediation, which has already failed twice in 2026, remains needed.

countries.name: India
incidents: 6 Jul — Ukhrul district, Manipur: Two Assam Rifles personnel were killed in an ambush on NH-202 near Noungshong Kong village; unverified claims point to a Naga insurgent faction (NSCN-IM 'Eastern Flank') following a mid-June internal mutiny manifesto.
Ongoing — LAC: China-India border delimitation talks continued without incident; the post-2025 thaw on boundary management remains intact.
Ongoing — Kashmir: No significant Line of Control incident was identified this week; the front remains quiet.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained, with a new internal watch item. Both the China border and the Pakistan Line of Control remain quiet, but the fatal Manipur ambush — tied to a reported fracture within a Naga insurgent group — is a genuinely new internal-security development distinct from the calmer external-border picture and should be tracked separately going forward.

countries.name: China
incidents: 6 Jul — South Pacific: China conducted its first-ever open-ocean submarine-launched ballistic missile test, firing roughly 7,300km from a PLAN submarine into the South Pacific Nuclear Free Zone; the missile likely overflew parts of the Philippines, drawing formal criticism from New Zealand, Australia, Japan and the US.

6-13 Jul — Yellow Sea/Qingdao: 'Joint Sea-2026' China-Russia live-fire naval exercises began, including joint reconnaissance, air/missile defense and submarine search-and-rescue drills, to be followed by joint Pacific patrols.

7 Jul — Senkaku Islands: Japanese and Chinese coast guards faced off after Japan says it expelled two Chinese vessels approaching a Japanese fishing boat — the second such confrontation since 10 June.
7 Jul — West Philippine Sea: 50+ Chinese vessels swarmed near Iroquois Reef and Sabina Shoal.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained, trajectory trending toward CRITICAL. The submarine-launched ballistic missile test is a significant new escalatory data point — the first demonstrated open-ocean nuclear-capable submarine launch — broadening China's coercive signaling from its immediate periphery to the wider Indo-Pacific. This is now a genuinely simultaneous five-front pressure campaign; a further activated front would warrant reassessment.

countries.name: Taiwan
incidents: 3 Jul — ADIZ: Taiwan detected 30 PLA aircraft sorties, 26 crossing the median line, across the northern, central, southwestern and eastern sectors.
4 Jul — ADIZ: 8 PLA sorties detected, 6 entering the southwestern ADIZ.
6 Jul — ADIZ: 5 PLA sorties detected, 3 entering the southwestern ADIZ.

3 Jul — Legislative Yuan: Four competing drone-procurement bills (Executive Yuan, KMT, TPP, DPP), in the NT$210-240bn range, were sent to joint committee review, a major domestic fight shaping Taiwan's asymmetric defense buildup.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. PLA ADIZ activity continues at a reduced-but-persistent pace, well below the 2024-25 peak but sustained; the drone-procurement fight is a notable domestic capability story worth tracking as it will shape Taiwan's future response posture.

countries.name: Philippines
incidents: 22 Jun (context, still shaping this week) — Scarborough Shoal: A rare direct naval standoff saw the frigate BRP Diego Silang confront four Chinese warships, with radio challenges exchanged on both sides.
7 Jul — Iroquois Reef/Sabina Shoal: 50+ Chinese vessels swarmed the area in the West Philippine Sea.

8 Jul — Spratly Islands: The Philippines formally protested Vietnamese island-building on Philippine-claimed reefs (Mascardo, Rizal, Juan Luna, Kota, Rajah Soliman), noting Vietnam has reclaimed roughly 1,121 hectares since March 2025 — opening a second maritime friction axis beyond China.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. China Coast Guard presence at Scarborough remains at record levels (933 ship-days in H1 2026, nearly matching all of 2025); a notable new wrinkle is the Philippines now also lodging formal protests against Vietnam over Spratly construction, diversifying its maritime friction beyond China alone.

countries.name: South Korea
incidents: 3 Jul — Sea of Japan/West Sea: North Korea's Kang Kon destroyer weapons tests were closely tracked by Seoul.
6-13 Jul — Yellow Sea: China-Russia 'Joint Sea-2026' drills near Qingdao remained within South Korea's monitoring range.

9 Jul — NATO summit, Turkey: Trump and President Lee agreed to deepen military shipbuilding cooperation; North Korea publicly condemned the deepening Seoul-Tokyo defense and logistics ties the same day.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. No new ADIZ scramble was confirmed strictly within this window, but the ongoing China-Russia Yellow Sea drill and North Korea's pointed reaction to Seoul-Tokyo cooperation keep tension elevated; sustained ADIZ response protocols remain warranted.

countries.name: Vietnam
incidents: 8 Jul — Spratly Islands: The Philippines formally protested Vietnamese construction on Philippine-claimed reefs; Mascardo Reef is now Vietnam's largest outpost, with Vietnam having reclaimed roughly 216 hectares since March 2025 (about 1,121 hectares cumulatively), shifting from land reclamation to operational infrastructure.
Ongoing (12-month data) — South China Sea: 100+ Chinese vessel transits (45+ coast guard/militia) within 10nm of Vietnamese-held features over the past year, nearly double the prior year's rate.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. No kinetic incident this week, but Vietnam's construction pace and the new Philippines-Vietnam friction axis over the Spratlys are a structural shift worth monitoring alongside continued Chinese pressure.

countries.name: Japan
incidents: 7 Jul — Senkaku Islands: Japanese and Chinese coast guards faced off after Japan says it expelled two Chinese vessels approaching a Japanese fishing boat — the second such incident since 10 June.
6 Jul — Pacific: Japan joined New Zealand, Australia and the US in formally objecting to China's submarine-launched ballistic missile test into the South Pacific Nuclear Free Zone.

Ongoing — China's export controls on 40 Japanese entities (including defense-research institutes and major manufacturers) remain in effect amid the broader China-Japan diplomatic crisis triggered by PM Takaichi's Taiwan remarks.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained, but pressure is compounding — Senkaku frictions, export controls, the SLBM objection and continued bomber-patrol responses are stacking within the same week. Watch for a possible upgrade if Senkaku incidents escalate further.

countries.name: Indonesia
incidents: 2 Jul (just before window, fallout continues) — Ipdeheik airstrip, Yahukimo, Papua: TPNPB-OPM militia shot and killed American missionary pilot Nikolas Gosselin and burned the aircraft — the first US citizen killed in the conflict.
2 Jul — Intan Jaya: A pregnant woman was killed in crossfire between TNI and TPNPB-OPM forces in Wandoga village, Sugapa district.

7 Jul — Jakarta: The Deputy Human Rights Minister announced the ministry will not form an investigative team to probe the Wandoga killing, drawing criticism from rights groups calling for an independent investigation.
3-9 Jul — Papua: TNI's Joint Regional Defense Command III announced plans to add troop reinforcements and security posts in vulnerable areas following the pilot killing.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Fallout from the American pilot's killing continues to drive troop reinforcement and a domestic accountability controversy over the government's refusal to investigate the Wandoga killing, which itself risks further radicalizing the situation. Expect increased US diplomatic engagement and monitor for further TPNPB attacks on civilian aircraft.

countries.name: Thailand
incidents: Ongoing — Thailand-Cambodia border: The ceasefire in effect since 27 December 2025 continues to hold; no new violation was identified this week, though roughly 32,000 displaced Cambodians along the border remain unable to return home.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. Ceasefire holding but underlying mistrust and a stalled Joint Boundary Commission process leave residual risk; resumption of JBC talks remains the key indicator to monitor.

countries.name: Bangladesh
incidents: 2 Jul — nationwide (June data): A Human Rights Support Society report tallied 9 killed and 346 injured across 58 incidents of political violence in June, including 8 BNP-Jamaat clashes.
29 Jun (just before window) — Gaibandha district: 8 injured in a clash between BNP and Jamaat-e-Islami/Islami Chhatra Shibir supporters in Jundah Bazar, Palashbari sub-district.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. Sporadic, low-casualty political clashes between the BNP and Jamaat-led alliance continue at a similar tempo to prior weeks; no escalatory trigger found this week.

countries.name: Sri Lanka
incidents: 5-7 Jul — Negombo Prison: A clash between rival drug-trafficking prison gangs escalated into inmates attacking intervening guards and attempting to breach the main gate; the death toll reached 26 (7 prison officials, 19 inmates) with 77-100+ injured, one of Sri Lanka's deadliest custodial incidents in over a decade.
6 Jul — nationwide: A revised bus-fare structure took effect with a 12-20% increase, an economic friction indicator.

Ongoing — Colombo: Sri Lanka's peacekeeping deployment of 1,000+ personnel to Haiti's Gang Suppression Force continues, notable given unresolved allegations of prior misconduct by Sri Lankan peacekeepers in earlier Haiti missions.
countries.coa: Upgraded to MODERATE. The Negombo Prison clash is a substantially larger-scale internal security failure than previously flagged custodial incidents, reflecting a significant breakdown in the corrections system even though it is not geopolitical or border-related; monitor for further unrest inside the prison system.

regions.name: Americas
overview: Colombia's presidential transition broke down entirely this week — outgoing President Petro disputed President-elect De la Espriella's election result, De la Espriella suspended the transition process and accused Petro of attempting a 'coup d'état,' while the ELN simultaneously mounted a sustained anniversary offensive (53 documented actions in six days) in Chocó — an unprecedented stacking of institutional and armed-group crises.

Venezuela's constitutional vacuum deepened rather than resolved — Delcy Rodríguez's 180-day interim mandate expired 4 July with total institutional silence, even as the earthquake death toll climbed past 3,500 and protesters gathered outside intelligence-agency headquarters demanding elections.

Cuba's energy crisis crossed from chronic to systemic collapse — a third total national blackout of 2026 (6 July, affecting 72% of the country) triggered renewed cacerolazo protests in Havana and Santiago even as Díaz-Canel conceded 'the worst has not happened yet.'

Peru's democratic transition proceeded cleanly (Fujimori formally proclaimed president-elect 3 July) even as the VRAEM state of emergency was extended rather than relaxed, underscoring that organized-crime and narcotrafficking threats are not improving even where electoral processes succeed.

countries.name: Venezuela
incidents: 3-5 Jul — Caracas: Interim President Delcy Rodríguez's 180-day constitutional mandate formally expired with neither the government nor the National Assembly issuing any statement on next steps; government legal teams reportedly weighed invoking natural-disaster emergency powers to justify an extra-constitutional extension.
3-7 Jul — nationwide: The earthquake death toll continued climbing — from 2,645 (3 Jul) to 3,535 dead, 16,740 injured and 17,854 homeless (7 Jul).

6-8 Jul — Caracas: Labor unions and civil-society groups marched demanding new presidential elections and criticizing the pace of quake response, gathering outside intelligence-agency (SEBIN) headquarters to demand release of political prisoners; organizers announced a follow-on nationwide protest for 28 July, the two-year mark since the disputed 2024 election.
6 Jul — El Callao: The mayor, a chavista-aligned local official, publicly stated Rodríguez's continued rule 'breaks the constitutional thread' — notable dissent from within the governing coalition.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, the constitutional vacuum is now unresolved rather than merely pending. The 180-day deadline has passed with total institutional silence, layered on a climbing death toll and reconstruction-driven protests explicitly demanding elections, including dissent from within chavismo itself. Oil infrastructure remains the one stabilizing factor; whether the government or Assembly moves to resolve the mandate question is the key 90-day indicator.

countries.name: Haiti
incidents: 4-7 Jul — Kenscoff, West Department: Viv Ansanm-affiliated gunmen mounted a renewed offensive attempting to retake the former TELECO facility from PNH/Task Force units, with clashes running overnight into midday 5 July and reported drone strikes on gang positions.
Ongoing — Port-au-Prince: At least 26 gangs continue to control roughly 90% of the capital; more than 2,300 killed and 1,100+ injured since the start of 2026, with 1.4 million displaced nationwide.

Ongoing — Gang Suppression Force: The GSF remains at only roughly 1,000 of its planned 5,500 personnel, still in early deployment with the UN Secretary-General's next report due 14 July ahead of a Security Council briefing.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Gangs contested territory against PNH/Task Force positions this week (Kenscoff) rather than showing any retreat, and the security force meant to reverse the crisis remains a fraction of planned strength heading into the 14 July UN briefing — the key date to watch for any change in international commitment.

countries.name: Colombia
incidents: 1-6 Jul — Chocó: The ELN mounted a sustained offensive timed to its 4 July anniversary, with authorities documenting 53 'terrorist actions' over six days including 16 drone attacks; three soldiers were wounded and the army says it neutralized 23 actions.
4 Jul — Tadó, Chocó: An ELN drone attack struck a police station using three explosive-laden drones in two separate strikes; no casualties reported.

6 Jul — National: President-elect Abelardo de la Espriella confirmed he will repeal Petro's 'Paz Total' policy effective 7 August and reactivate all suspended arrest warrants against armed-group members.

7-8 Jul — National: Outgoing President Petro publicly disputed the legitimacy of De la Espriella's election, alleging unproven 'algorithmic manipulation' in overseas voting; De la Espriella suspended the presidential transition process entirely, accusing Petro of attempting a 'coup d'état,' as international observers backed the election's legitimacy and the Comptroller General convened emergency mediation.
countries.coa: Upgraded to CRITICAL. Colombia now has two crises stacking simultaneously — an active ELN military offensive in Chocó and an unprecedented breakdown of the presidential transition, with the outgoing president disputing the election result and the transition process itself suspended. This combination of armed-group escalation and institutional/succession crisis is a significant deterioration from the prior HIGH assessment; resolution of the transition dispute over the coming days is the critical near-term indicator.

countries.name: Cuba
incidents: 2 Jul — Havana: Díaz-Canel stated Cuba 'does not seek war with the US' but is preparing for possible aggression, accusing Washington of 'psychological war' amid sanctions and the energy crisis.

6 Jul — nationwide: A total collapse of the National Electric System — the third full national blackout of 2026 and eighth since October 2024 — left some Matanzas circuits without power for up to 87 consecutive hours and affected roughly 72% of national territory.

6-7 Jul — Havana/Santiago de Cuba: Renewed cacerolazo protests broke out in multiple Havana neighborhoods and in Santiago, met with police dispersal operations; the Cuban Conflict Observatory recorded 220 public protests in June alone.
7 Jul — nationwide: The Cuban Observatory of Human Rights reported nearly 1,949 repressive actions in H1 2026, including 257 arbitrary detentions.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The energy crisis has crossed from chronic to systemic collapse — three total national grid failures in 2026 alone — while protests continue spreading and Díaz-Canel himself has publicly conceded 'the worst has not happened yet.' The root cause, the US blockade on Venezuelan oil shipments, remains unresolved.

countries.name: Ecuador
incidents: 5 Jul — Guayaquil: An armed attack at the '100% Salsa Sur' nightclub killed 3 (including a 16-year-old) and wounded 3; investigators point to a dispute between criminal organizations.
Late Jun-early Jul — El Oro province: 489 violent deaths were recorded in H1 2026, including 42 murdered women, already matching all of 2025's provincial total.

Ongoing — nationwide: The 60-day state of exception covering 10 provinces plus 3 cantons remains in force through mid-August, alongside a decree granting immunity to foreign troops cooperating against organized crime following Noboa's meeting with US Defense Secretary Hegseth.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. No single mass-casualty event this week matched the prior week's 15-killings-in-3-days streak, but underlying conditions — record homicide trajectory, an active state of exception, and expanding foreign military cooperation — are unchanged or intensifying; track whether the state of emergency reduces the homicide rate or merely displaces violence.

countries.name: Peru
incidents: 3 Jul — Lima: Peru's National Electoral Jury formally proclaimed Keiko Fujimori president-elect for the 2026-2031 term, making her the first woman elected by popular vote to govern Peru.

6 Jul — nationwide: The government extended the state of emergency in Ayacucho, Cusco and Junín (VRAEM corridor) citing continued expansion of drug-trafficking networks; prosecutors have identified 65 family clans running trafficking operations across 8 regions.
Upcoming — Lima: A formal credentialing ceremony is scheduled for 15 July, ahead of the 28 July inauguration.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. The democratic transition itself is proceeding cleanly — the proclamation was completed without incident — but the parallel VRAEM security emergency was extended rather than relaxed, underscoring that the organized-crime threat is not improving even as the political transition succeeds; 15 and 28 July remain the next flashpoints to watch for potential unrest.

countries.name: Bolivia
incidents: 1 Jul — La Paz: President Rodrigo Paz announced a package of decrees intended to reactivate sectors economically damaged by the seven-week blockade crisis.

2 Jul — nationwide: A Human Rights Watch report, 'Bolivia Cleared the Roads, but Hasn't Cleared the Crisis,' documented severe medicine and food shortages during the blockades and at least one death during police attempts to open humanitarian corridors, stressing the underlying justice-system dysfunction remains unresolved even though roads are now clear.

Early Jul — nationwide: The Ministry of Defense estimated 91 million bolivianos in road-infrastructure damage, at least 10 dead, 37 injured and 360+ detained cumulatively from the blockade period; a 90-day state of emergency banning blockades remains in force.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Roads are physically clear and the government is in reactivation mode, but HRW's framing — crisis contained, not resolved — argues against downgrading; watch whether COB/sectoral dialogue tables produce a durable settlement or another blockade wave.

countries.name: Dominican Republic
incidents: 1 Jul — nationwide: Dominican migration authorities reported deporting 34,226 Haitians in June alone, bringing the January-June 2026 total to 196,321 as part of a policy that has deported 670,500 Haitians since October 2024.

Early Jul — Elías Piña/Dajabón: The Dominican Army Commander toured border units, stating coordination with Haiti's Gang Suppression Force is 'advancing positively' and that the border remains under elevated security control.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. No security incident this week, only continued high-volume administrative deportation and routine border-security messaging; the second-order Haiti/TPS-driven migration pressure remains a watch item rather than a materialized incident.

countries.name: Jamaica
incidents: 5-6 Jul — nationwide: The National Security Minister reported a 24% year-on-year decline in murders as of 5 July (275 in 2026 vs. 362 at the same point in 2025) and a 27% decline in shootings.
8 Jul — nationwide: The Jamaica Constabulary Force reported its murder clearance rate climbed to 111%, with 304 cases cleared 1 January-4 July 2026 versus 253 in the same period last year.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. The positive crime-reduction trend continues to strengthen, reinforced by new July figures; direct targeted resources to any parishes still bucking the national downward trend.
